title: Capstone 12 — Video Understanding Pipeline (Scene, QA, Search)

questions.question: Why is a scene-level index still needed even when a long-context VLM can read a 2-hour video natively?
options: Long-context VLMs cannot stream output
Vector indexes do not support video files
Ingesting 100 hours of video into a queryable corpus needs scene-level retrieval, even when individual videos can be read whole
Scene cuts are the only thing a VLM understands

questions.question: Which three vector types does each scene get in the multi-vector index?
options: Caption embedding, keyframe embedding, and transcript embedding
Audio waveform, mel-spectrogram, and MFCCs
Hash, gzip, and CRC
Pose, depth, and optical flow

questions.question: How are results from the three retrieval streams merged at query time?
options: By picking the caption hit only
By averaging cosine similarities
Via reciprocal rank fusion across the three ranked lists
By taking the union without scoring

questions.question: What does the temporal grounding step refine inside the top scene?
options: The transcript word timestamps from Whisper
The number of vectors stored in Qdrant
The (start, end) timestamp window that contains the answer
The keyframe embedding dimensionality

questions.question: Which question class is reported separately because it is a known hallucination hotspot?
options: Descriptive questions about scenery
Speaker identification questions
Translation questions
Counting and action-type questions, where VLMs miscount or mis-order events

questions.question: Which grounding metric does the rubric measure on a held-out set?
options: Temporal-grounding intersection-over-union (IoU)
Acceptance rate
Perplexity
MRR@10

questions.question: Why does the pipeline require cited timestamps in answers?
options: They reduce the embedding dimension
Timestamps are required by Whisper output
Timestamps are required by Qdrant payloads
They let the viewer jump to the exact (video_id, start, end) so users can verify the claim
